headline: Nussmeier's precision overcomes Oregon's yardage advantage in thriller
body: LSU's Garrett Nussmeier delivered when it mattered most, completing 16 of 29 passes for 212 yards and three touchdowns to lead the Tigers past Oregon 27-24 in a game that unfolded as a study in efficiency versus volume. Oregon dominated the stat sheet with 355 total yards to LSU's 250, but couldn't convert that advantage into points, largely because the Ducks couldn't sustain drives when it counted. Nussmeier's three touchdown passes, anchored by receiver Cake's seven-catch, 117-yard performance including two scores, gave LSU the margin of victory despite being outgained significantly.

The Oregon passing game looked explosive on paper: Dan Thomas connected for 236 yards and three touchdowns across 42 attempts. But that completion percentage tells the real story. Thomas worked behind a line that allowed Mothudi to rack up three sacks, and while Laquon Bryant's 70-yard receiving day suggested deep shots were working, the Ducks couldn't string together the kind of consistent scoring drives that would have put LSU away. Oregon's ground game, led by Hughes with 49 yards on 11 carries, never threatened, and the burden fell entirely on Thomas's arm.

LSU's defense, anchored by Wood's eight tackles, bent without breaking when it absolutely had to. The Tigers forced no turnovers and gave up no sacks, a stability that allowed Nussmeier to operate with confidence in the pocket. That clean pocket was the difference. Oregon's weapons are real, but LSU's decision-making and execution in the red zone proved superior when the scoreboard mattered most.
turningPoint: Cake's second touchdown reception gave LSU a 27-17 lead with enough time remaining for Oregon to threaten but not recover, as the Ducks' next three possessions failed to produce points.

unsungHero.name: Wood
unsungHero.why: Eight tackles anchored a defensive effort that allowed LSU to win despite being outgained by over 100 yards; his presence in the middle kept Oregon's drive-extending plays to a minimum and gave Nussmeier time to operate.
